title: Ankylosing Spondylitis
oneLiner: Chronic progressive inflammatory arthritis of sacroiliac joints and spine causing progressive fusion; young men present with morning back pain.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cauda equina syndrome** — rare but catastrophic if missed. *Finding:* acute bilateral leg weakness/sciatica, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention.
- **Spinal fracture** — fused spine is brittle; minimal trauma can cause unstable fracture (especially cervical). *Finding:* acute onset severe neck/back pain after fall or even coughing, with neurological deficit.
- **Acute anterior uveitis** — can cause permanent vision loss within hours if untreated. *Finding:* acute painful red eye with photophobia, blurred vision.

**Rule out these three BEFORE treating as routine flare.**
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Inflammatory back pain: insidious onset, morning stiffness >30 minutes, improves with exercise / worsens with rest, night pain (often wakens in second half of night)
  - Enthesitis: heel pain (Achilles/plantar fascia), chest wall pain (costosternal)
  - Peripheral arthritis: hips, shoulders (large joints); less commonly knees/ankles
  - Extra-articular: uveitis, psoriasis, IBD symptoms (colitis)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced spinal flexion (Schober test — <5 cm expansion)
  - Reduced lateral spinal flexion
  - Reduced chest expansion (<2.5 cm at nipple line)
  - Occiput-to-wall distance >0 (late sign)
  - Sacroiliac joint tenderness (FABER test — pain on flexion, abduction, external rotation)
  - ± Peripheral joint effusion, entheseal tenderness
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Mechanical back pain** — no morning stiffness, pain with activity, not at rest, normal Schober/chest expansion
- **Other spondyloarthropathies** (psoriatic, reactive, IBD-related) — differentiate by extra-articular features (psoriasis, nail pitting, conjunctivitis, diarrhoea)
- **Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH)** — more anterior flowing ossification, no SI joint involvement, no inflammatory back pain
- **Sacroiliitis from infection or neoplasia** — fever, localised severe pain, systemic symptoms
- **Splitter:** *Improves with exercise + morning stiffness >30 min* → inflammatory arthritis; *worse with activity + no morning stiffness* → mechanical.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Schober test, chest expansion, occiput-to-wall
  - Urgent slit-lamp eye exam if visual/eye symptoms present
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR, LFTs, U&Es, bone profile
  - HLA-B27 (positive in ~90% of white patients; not diagnostic alone)
- **Imaging:**
  - *First-line:* Plain AP pelvis (sacroiliitis: sclerosis, erosions, fusion; also detect hip disease)
  - *Second-line:* MRI SI joints with STIR (early sacroiliitis; subtle changes before X-ray)
  - *If spinal fracture suspected:* CT spine (MRI if neurological deficit)
- **Special tests:** None routinely required in ED/ward
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - ASAS classification criteria (for axial spondyloarthritis): ≥3 months back pain + age <45 + either (1) sacroiliitis on imaging + ≥1 SpA feature OR (2) HLA-B27 + ≥2 SpA features (e.g., inflammatory back pain, uveitis, dactylitis, etc.)
  - Modified New York criteria (definite AS): sacroiliitis grade ≥2 bilaterally or ≥3 unilaterally on X-ray + one clinical criterion (low back pain >3 months, limited lumbar spine motion, reduced chest expansion)
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Slit lamp eye exam** in any patient with red/painful eye → uveitis
  - **MRI SI joints** if clinical suspicion high but plain X-ray normal (especially in young patients with inflammatory back pain <10 years duration)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If uveitis: urgent ophthalmology review, topical steroids + mydriatics
  - If spinal fracture: immobilise with cervical collar / spinal precautions, CT imaging, neurosurgical referral
  - If cauda equina: urgent MRI spine + neurosurgical referral
  - Analgesia: NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen 500mg PO) — ensure no contraindications (GI bleed, CKD, pregnancy)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue NSAIDs (with PPI cover if high GI risk)
  - Start physiotherapy: spinal extension exercises, deep breathing
  - Assess disease activity: BASDAI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Index) – score ≥4 suggests high activity
  - Consider TNF inhibitor if active disease not responding to NSAIDs (e.g., adalimumab, etanercept) — discuss with rheumatology; do not start without expert opinion
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Regular physiotherapy (pool/land-based)
  - NSAIDs as required
  - TNF inhibitors (if active axial disease) or IL-17 inhibitors (e.g., secukinumab) – specialist only
  - Annual monitoring: clinical status, BASDAI, CRP, chest X-ray (for apical fibrosis), ECG (for conduction defects), echo (for aortic regurgitation)
- **Default Orders (if admitted for flare or complication):**
  - Fluids: oral unless NBM; no specific IV fluid requirement
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider if reduced mobility (LMWH as per local policy)
  - Diet: normal
  - Observations: routine NEWS2 unless unstable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent:**
  - **Naproxen** 500mg twice daily PO (with food) — OR **Indomethacin** (immediate release 25-50mg three times daily, or modified release 75mg once daily)
  - Duration: until symptoms controlled, then lowest effective dose
- **Common alternative:**
  - **Etoricoxib** 90mg once daily (COX‑2 inhibitor; lower GI risk but higher cardiovascular caution)
  - **Sulfasalazine** (for peripheral arthritis) – 500mg twice daily increasing to 1g twice daily (check local protocol)
- **Biologics (refer to rheumatology):**
  - Adalimumab 40mg subcut every 2 weeks or etanercept 50mg subcut weekly — consult local guidelines for dosing
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if GFR <30, active peptic ulcer, known aspirin-allergic asthma
  - TNF inhibitors: screen for latent TB (chest X-ray, IGRA) before starting; avoid in active infection, demyelinating disease, heart failure (NYHA III–IV)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: reduce dose in CKD, avoid if severe renal impairment
  - Sulfasalazine: reduce dose in hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NSAIDs in renal impairment — always check U&Es before prescribing. Missed acute kidney injury can lead to irreversible damage.**
  - **Biologics – missing TB screening before starting is potentially fatal (reactivation TB).**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Acute anterior uveitis (up to 40% lifetime risk)
  - Spinal fracture (often unrecognised)
  - Cauda equina syndrome
  - Myocardial infarction (increased risk with inflammation)
- **Chronic:**
  - Kyphosis, restrictive lung disease (apical fibrosis)
  - Aortic regurgitation (aortitis)
  - Cardiac conduction defects (heart block)
  - Amyloidosis (AA type – rare)
  - Osteoporosis (increased fracture risk)
  - Atlantoaxial subluxation
sections.Prognosis: - Majority have intermittent flares but preserve function with treatment. ~20% develop severe spinal fusion causing disability. Mortality slightly increased due to cardiovascular disease and respiratory complications; early treatment improves outcomes.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Use BASDAI** to objectively assess disease activity before escalating therapy (score 0-10; ≥4 = active)
- **Always perform chest expansion measurement** — it’s quick, cheap, and tracks disease progression
- **Ask about eye symptoms** on every visit — uveitis is the most common extra-articular feature and requires urgent ophthalmology
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don't rely on normal sacroiliac X-ray** in young patients with classic symptoms — MRI can show active inflammation years before X-ray changes
- **Don't prescribe NSAIDs without checking renal function** — many patients have undiagnosed CKD
- **Don't miss acute uveitis** — a red eye that is painful with photophobia is an emergency, not a side issue
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New eye pain/redness/blurring → immediate A&E/ophthalmology
  - Sudden severe back/neck pain after any trauma (even minor) → needs spinal imaging
  - New leg weakness, numbness, or bladder/bowel dysfunction → possible cauda equina
- **Follow-up:**
  - Routine: rheumatology clinic within 4-6 weeks for new diagnosis
  - For confirmed AS: annual rheumatology review; physiotherapy referral
  - Eye symptoms: refer to ophthalmology same day
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - New focal neurology or cauda equina signs → urgent MRI
  - Suspected spinal fracture → immobilise, CT, neurosurgery referral
  - Acute uveitis unresponsive to first-line drops
- **Critical care (ICU) triggers:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Septic shock (if infected joint or urosepsis from NSAID-induced AKI)
  - Unstable spinal fracture with neurological compromise
  - Arrhythmias from cardiac involvement (heart block)
- **Always escalate if:** suspected cauda equina syndrome — this is a time-critical neurosurgical emergency.

rawMarkdown:
## Ankylosing Spondylitis
**One-liner:** Chronic progressive inflammatory arthritis of sacroiliac joints and spine causing progressive fusion; young men present with morning back pain.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cauda equina syndrome** — rare but catastrophic if missed. *Finding:* acute bilateral leg weakness/sciatica, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention.
- **Spinal fracture** — fused spine is brittle; minimal trauma can cause unstable fracture (especially cervical). *Finding:* acute onset severe neck/back pain after fall or even coughing, with neurological deficit.
- **Acute anterior uveitis** — can cause permanent vision loss within hours if untreated. *Finding:* acute painful red eye with photophobia, blurred vision.

**Rule out these three BEFORE treating as routine flare.**

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Inflammatory back pain: insidious onset, morning stiffness >30 minutes, improves with exercise / worsens with rest, night pain (often wakens in second half of night)
  - Enthesitis: heel pain (Achilles/plantar fascia), chest wall pain (costosternal)
  - Peripheral arthritis: hips, shoulders (large joints); less commonly knees/ankles
  - Extra-articular: uveitis, psoriasis, IBD symptoms (colitis)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced spinal flexion (Schober test — <5 cm expansion)
  - Reduced lateral spinal flexion
  - Reduced chest expansion (<2.5 cm at nipple line)
  - Occiput-to-wall distance >0 (late sign)
  - Sacroiliac joint tenderness (FABER test — pain on flexion, abduction, external rotation)
  - ± Peripheral joint effusion, entheseal tenderness

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Mechanical back pain** — no morning stiffness, pain with activity, not at rest, normal Schober/chest expansion
- **Other spondyloarthropathies** (psoriatic, reactive, IBD-related) — differentiate by extra-articular features (psoriasis, nail pitting, conjunctivitis, diarrhoea)
- **Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH)** — more anterior flowing ossification, no SI joint involvement, no inflammatory back pain
- **Sacroiliitis from infection or neoplasia** — fever, localised severe pain, systemic symptoms
- **Splitter:** *Improves with exercise + morning stiffness >30 min* → inflammatory arthritis; *worse with activity + no morning stiffness* → mechanical.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Schober test, chest expansion, occiput-to-wall
  - Urgent slit-lamp eye exam if visual/eye symptoms present
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR, LFTs, U&Es, bone profile
  - HLA-B27 (positive in ~90% of white patients; not diagnostic alone)
- **Imaging:**
  - *First-line:* Plain AP pelvis (sacroiliitis: sclerosis, erosions, fusion; also detect hip disease)
  - *Second-line:* MRI SI joints with STIR (early sacroiliitis; subtle changes before X-ray)
  - *If spinal fracture suspected:* CT spine (MRI if neurological deficit)
- **Special tests:** None routinely required in ED/ward
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - ASAS classification criteria (for axial spondyloarthritis): ≥3 months back pain + age <45 + either (1) sacroiliitis on imaging + ≥1 SpA feature OR (2) HLA-B27 + ≥2 SpA features (e.g., inflammatory back pain, uveitis, dactylitis, etc.)
  - Modified New York criteria (definite AS): sacroiliitis grade ≥2 bilaterally or ≥3 unilaterally on X-ray + one clinical criterion (low back pain >3 months, limited lumbar spine motion, reduced chest expansion)
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Slit lamp eye exam** in any patient with red/painful eye → uveitis
  - **MRI SI joints** if clinical suspicion high but plain X-ray normal (especially in young patients with inflammatory back pain <10 years duration)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If uveitis: urgent ophthalmology review, topical steroids + mydriatics
  - If spinal fracture: immobilise with cervical collar / spinal precautions, CT imaging, neurosurgical referral
  - If cauda equina: urgent MRI spine + neurosurgical referral
  - Analgesia: NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen 500mg PO) — ensure no contraindications (GI bleed, CKD, pregnancy)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue NSAIDs (with PPI cover if high GI risk)
  - Start physiotherapy: spinal extension exercises, deep breathing
  - Assess disease activity: BASDAI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Index) – score ≥4 suggests high activity
  - Consider TNF inhibitor if active disease not responding to NSAIDs (e.g., adalimumab, etanercept) — discuss with rheumatology; do not start without expert opinion
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Regular physiotherapy (pool/land-based)
  - NSAIDs as required
  - TNF inhibitors (if active axial disease) or IL-17 inhibitors (e.g., secukinumab) – specialist only
  - Annual monitoring: clinical status, BASDAI, CRP, chest X-ray (for apical fibrosis), ECG (for conduction defects), echo (for aortic regurgitation)
- **Default Orders (if admitted for flare or complication):**
  - Fluids: oral unless NBM; no specific IV fluid requirement
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider if reduced mobility (LMWH as per local policy)
  - Diet: normal
  - Observations: routine NEWS2 unless unstable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent:**
  - **Naproxen** 500mg twice daily PO (with food) — OR **Indomethacin** (immediate release 25-50mg three times daily, or modified release 75mg once daily)
  - Duration: until symptoms controlled, then lowest effective dose
- **Common alternative:**
  - **Etoricoxib** 90mg once daily (COX‑2 inhibitor; lower GI risk but higher cardiovascular caution)
  - **Sulfasalazine** (for peripheral arthritis) – 500mg twice daily increasing to 1g twice daily (check local protocol)
- **Biologics (refer to rheumatology):**
  - Adalimumab 40mg subcut every 2 weeks or etanercept 50mg subcut weekly — consult local guidelines for dosing
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if GFR <30, active peptic ulcer, known aspirin-allergic asthma
  - TNF inhibitors: screen for latent TB (chest X-ray, IGRA) before starting; avoid in active infection, demyelinating disease, heart failure (NYHA III–IV)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - NSAIDs: reduce dose in CKD, avoid if severe renal impairment
  - Sulfasalazine: reduce dose in hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NSAIDs in renal impairment — always check U&Es before prescribing. Missed acute kidney injury can lead to irreversible damage.**
  - **Biologics – missing TB screening before starting is potentially fatal (reactivation TB).**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Acute anterior uveitis (up to 40% lifetime risk)
  - Spinal fracture (often unrecognised)
  - Cauda equina syndrome
  - Myocardial infarction (increased risk with inflammation)
- **Chronic:**
  - Kyphosis, restrictive lung disease (apical fibrosis)
  - Aortic regurgitation (aortitis)
  - Cardiac conduction defects (heart block)
  - Amyloidosis (AA type – rare)
  - Osteoporosis (increased fracture risk)
  - Atlantoaxial subluxation

### Prognosis
- Majority have intermittent flares but preserve function with treatment. ~20% develop severe spinal fusion causing disability. Mortality slightly increased due to cardiovascular disease and respiratory complications; early treatment improves outcomes.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Use BASDAI** to objectively assess disease activity before escalating therapy (score 0-10; ≥4 = active)
- **Always perform chest expansion measurement** — it’s quick, cheap, and tracks disease progression
- **Ask about eye symptoms** on every visit — uveitis is the most common extra-articular feature and requires urgent ophthalmology

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don't rely on normal sacroiliac X-ray** in young patients with classic symptoms — MRI can show active inflammation years before X-ray changes
- **Don't prescribe NSAIDs without checking renal function** — many patients have undiagnosed CKD
- **Don't miss acute uveitis** — a red eye that is painful with photophobia is an emergency, not a side issue

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New eye pain/redness/blurring → immediate A&E/ophthalmology
  - Sudden severe back/neck pain after any trauma (even minor) → needs spinal imaging
  - New leg weakness, numbness, or bladder/bowel dysfunction → possible cauda equina
- **Follow-up:**
  - Routine: rheumatology clinic within 4-6 weeks for new diagnosis
  - For confirmed AS: annual rheumatology review; physiotherapy referral
  - Eye symptoms: refer to ophthalmology same day

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - New focal neurology or cauda equina signs → urgent MRI
  - Suspected spinal fracture → immobilise, CT, neurosurgery referral
  - Acute uveitis unresponsive to first-line drops
- **Critical care (ICU) triggers:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Septic shock (if infected joint or urosepsis from NSAID-induced AKI)
  - Unstable spinal fracture with neurological compromise
  - Arrhythmias from cardiac involvement (heart block)
- **Always escalate if:** suspected cauda equina syndrome — this is a time-critical neurosurgical emergency.
